ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Manchow Soup; mapped to broader 'soup food product' class. Manchow Soup is an Indo-Chinese vegetable soup with crispy noodle garnish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Manchow Soup; mapped to 'Soups and broths' (A00GJ) as the closest defensible category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Manchow Soup or Indo-Chinese soup category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Manchow Soup is approximately 300 g, typically served as a starter or side dish at an Indo-Chinese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Manchow Soup is not listed as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods. Nutrient values are estimated from component ingredients (cabbage, carrots, bell peppers, ginger, garlic, soy sauce, cornstarch, vegetable oil) using ICMR-NIN tables for individual ingredients and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cross-validation. Values represent a typical vegetable Manchow Soup without the fried noodle garnish unless otherwise noted. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for vegetable soup analogues.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Manchow Soup. GI of 50 is estimated by analogy to vegetable soups thickened with cornstarch, which fall in the low-to-medium GI range (GI ~40–55) per University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable soups and cornstarch-thickened broths. The fried noodle garnish, if included, would raise the effective GI. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 21.99) / 100 ≈ 10.99, based on 300 g serving with 7.33 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (400 mg per 100 g; ~1200 mg per 300 g bowl) from soy sauce and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Manchow Soup due to its very high sodium load; if consumed, request reduced soy sauce and no added salt, and limit portion to no more than 150 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content (7.33 g per 100 g) with low GI (~50); fried noodle garnish adds high-GI refined carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: Manchow Soup without the crispy noodle garnish is acceptable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions; the fried noodle garnish should be omitted or strictly limited to avoid glycemic spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content raises blood pressure risk; low saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g) and zero cholesterol are favorable
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (400 mg per 100 g) is the primary cardiovascular concern; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request a low-sodium preparation and avoid the fried noodle garnish to minimize saturated fat and refined carbohydrate intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory ginger and garlic are beneficial; high sodium and fried noodles may be pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ginger and garlic in Manchow Soup provide anti-inflammatory compounds; however, the high sodium content and fried noodle garnish may exacerbate inflammation and should be limited.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Manchow Soup is a popular Indo-Chinese soup originating in India, characterized by its dark, rich broth, bold spicy flavor, and the signature garnish of crispy deep-fried noodles. The soup base is prepared by sautéing finely chopped vegetables — typically cabbage, carrots, and bell peppers — with ginger, garlic, and green chilies in oil, then simmering in a seasoned broth flavored with soy sauce and sometimes vinegar or chili sauce. The soup is thickened to a light, glossy consistency using a cornstarch slurry. It is served piping hot, topped with a generous handful of crispy fried noodles that provide textural contrast. Nutritionally, Manchow Soup is relatively low in calories (approximately 60 kcal per 100 g) and low in fat (2.67 g per 100 g), making it a lighter starter option. However, its sodium content is very high at 400 mg per 100 g, driven by soy sauce and added salt, which is a significant concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The soup provides modest amounts of vitamin A (100 mcg RAE per 100 g) from the carrots and bell peppers, and small amounts of vitamin C, iron, and dietary fibre. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 50 (low) for the soup base, though the fried noodle garnish adds high-GI refined carbohydrates. Manchow Soup is typically served as a starter or side dish at lunch or dinner in Indian restaurants and is a staple of the Indo-Chinese culinary tradition, which blends Chinese cooking techniques with Indian spices and flavors.

confidence_reason: Manchow Soup is not listed as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from component ingredients using ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy tables. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable soups. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and variability in restaurant preparations.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for Manchow Soup is not available in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central for this composite dish as of 2024. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite soup preparation is not reported in the consulted approved sources. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy vegetable soup analogues). 4. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero — this is a vegan vegetable soup with no animal-derived ingredients). 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is reported as 0.0 (naturally zero — this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy). 6. Nutrient values represent the soup base without the fried noodle garnish unless otherwise noted; the garnish adds additional calories, carbohydrates, and fat. 7. Portions in the input contained three duplicate entries for '1 serving (250ml)'; deduplicated to one entry. The default serving used is 1 bowl (300 g). 8. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Manchow Soup was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024.
